question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send duplicate query keys differing only in case (e.g., 'TIMESTAMP=gt:100&timestamp=lt:200') so that requestQueryParser() merges them into an array under 'timestamp', causing downstream range filters to silently drop records that fall within the intended range, resulting in missing transaction records returned to the user?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: merge()] Can an unprivileged external user send a repeated query parameter (e.g., 'order=asc&ORDER=desc') so that merge() combines them into an array ['asc','desc'] under the key 'order', causing parseLimitAndOrderParams() to fall back to the default sort order instead of the user-specified one, resulting in records being returned in an incorrect order that omits boundary records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the case-insensitive key normalization in requestQueryParser() by sending 'Result=success&RESULT=fail' so that both values are merged under 'result', causing the SQL filter to include both success and fail transactions when only one was intended, returning incorrect records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: canonicalizeValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send a mixed-case value for a non-canonicalized filter key (e.g., 'token.type=FUNGIBLE_COMMON') that bypasses canonicalization since only 'order' and 'result' are in queryCanonicalizationMap, causing the value to be passed as-is to downstream validators that may accept or reject it inconsistently, leading to missing token records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send 'limit=100&LIMIT=1' so that requestQueryParser() merges them into an array ['100','1'] under 'limit', causing getLimitParamValue() to honor only the last element ('1') and return far fewer records than expected, effectively hiding records from the user?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send 'account.id=0.0.100&ACCOUNT.ID=0.0.200' so that requestQueryParser() merges them under 'account.id' as an array, causing the downstream SQL IN() clause to include both accounts when only one was intended, returning records belonging to a different account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a query string where a key appears with multiple case variants (e.g., 'Timestamp=gt:100&TIMESTAMP=lt:200&timestamp=eq:150') so that requestQueryParser() merges all three into an array, causing the timestamp filter to produce a contradictory SQL clause that returns zero records (missing all records)?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query parameter with a key that, after lowercasing, collides with an internal or reserved key name, causing requestQueryParser() to inject an unexpected filter into the query object that silently excludes records from the mirror node response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the qs.parse() call with queryOptions (depth:1, strictDepth:true) by sending a nested parameter like 'timestamp[gt]=100' that qs parses as an object {gt:'100'} at depth 1, which then passes through requestQueryParser() without canonicalization and reaches downstream validators in an unexpected object form, causing the timestamp filter to be silently ignored and all records to be returned regardless of time range?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a parameter at depth 2 (e.g., 'a[b][c]=1') that causes qs.parse() to throw due to strictDepth:true, resulting in an unhandled exception that bypasses all query filters and returns an incorrect or empty record set?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send exactly maxRepeatedQueryParameters+1 values for a single parameter (e.g., 'timestamp=gt:1&timestamp=gt:2&...') to trigger throwOnLimitExceeded:true in qs.parse(), causing an unhandled error that results in a response with no records instead of a proper validation error?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string where the same filter key appears both as a plain key and as an array-encoded key (e.g., 'timestamp=gt:100&timestamp[]=lt:200') so that qs.parse() produces a mixed structure that requestQueryParser() merges incorrectly, causing one of the timestamp bounds to be dropped and records outside the intended range to be returned?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: canonicalizeValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send 'order=ASC' (uppercase) so that canonicalizeValue() applies lowerCaseQueryValue and produces 'asc', but simultaneously send 'ORDER=DESC' so that after key lowercasing and merging, the array ['asc','desc'] is produced, causing the order filter to be ambiguous and records to be returned in an unspecified order that may omit pagination boundary records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that requestLogger() sets res.locals.statusCode to httpStatusCodes.OK.code by default, so that if a downstream error causes partial record retrieval, the response still reports 200 OK, masking the fact that records are missing from the mirror node export?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestLogger()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of concurrent requests to cause randomString(8) to generate colliding request IDs stored via httpContext.set(requestIdLabel, requestId), leading to request context cross-contamination where one request's query filters are applied to another request's database query, returning incorrect records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a URL-encoded query string where a key decodes to an empty string after lowercasing (e.g., '%00=value'), causing requestQueryParser() to insert an entry with an empty key into caseInsensitiveQueryString, which downstream validators do not recognize and silently ignore, allowing unvalidated filter values to reach the database query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a key that contains Unicode characters that lowercase differently across locales (e.g., Turkish dotless-i), causing requestQueryParser()'s key.toLowerCase() to produce a key that does not match any expected filter key, silently dropping the filter and returning all records instead of the filtered subset?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: merge()] Can an unprivileged external user send a parameter value that is itself an array-encoded string (e.g., 'timestamp[0]=gt:100&timestamp[1]=lt:200') so that qs.parse() at depth:1 produces an array object, and merge() then spreads it into the existing array using push(...next), causing duplicate or out-of-order filter values that produce an incorrect SQL WHERE clause returning wrong records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a very long key name (e.g., thousands of characters) that, after lowercasing, does not match any known filter key, causing requestQueryParser() to silently include it in caseInsensitiveQueryString, and if downstream code iterates over all keys without strict allowlisting, the unknown key could interfere with SQL query construction and cause missing records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string where a parameter key is '__proto__' or 'constructor' so that after qs.parse() and the key.toLowerCase() normalization, requestQueryParser() inserts it into caseInsensitiveQueryString via bracket notation, potentially polluting the object prototype and causing all subsequent filter lookups to return unexpected values, leading to incorrect records being exported?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send 'result=success&Result=fail' so that requestQueryParser() first processes 'result' (lowercased to 'result', canonicalized to 'success'), then processes 'Result' (lowercased to 'result', canonicalized to 'fail'), and since 'result' already exists in caseInsensitiveQueryString, merge() combines them into ['success','fail'], causing the result filter to return both successful and failed transactions when only successful ones were requested?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string where the 'order' parameter appears multiple times with different cases (e.g., 'order=asc&Order=asc&ORDER=asc') so that merge() creates an array ['asc','asc','asc'], and downstream parseLimitAndOrderParams() receives an array instead of a string, causing it to use the default order (desc) instead of the requested ascending order, resulting in records being returned in reverse order with incorrect pagination?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: canonicalizeValue()] Can an unprivileged external user send an array value for the 'result' parameter (e.g., 'result=success&result=FAIL') so that canonicalizeValue() applies value.map(v => lowerCaseQueryValue(v)) to produce ['success','fail'], and downstream code that only expects a single result value receives an array, causing it to either ignore the filter entirely or apply it incorrectly, returning records with wrong result status?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with a null byte in a parameter value (e.g., 'timestamp=gt:100%00extra') so that qs.parse() includes the null byte in the value, which then passes through requestQueryParser() without sanitization, causing the downstream timestamp validator to reject it and silently drop the filter, returning all records regardless of timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/requestHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: requestQueryParser()] Can an unprivileged external user send a query string with an extremely large number of unique parameter keys (e.g., 500 different keys) so that requestQueryParser() iterates over all of them and inserts them into caseInsensitiveQueryString, causing downstream validateReq() to generate a large badParams array that triggers an error response, resulting in no records being returned instead of a partial filtered result?
